sentences.text: No, well, but there-- Over there, the rumors was coming, a lot of people.
And people wasn't the freedom.
They was working in the street, but in the stores, was not too much.
All the restaurants was open, and people was living not bad.
Only, like I did, little bit black market.
In the meantime-- was over there tow truck, like Maxwell Street.
You hear about that with Chicago?
I went to Vilna.
I went to Brest.
I bought something over there, and I went out in this little truck to sell it.
One time, the police got me.
I have some-- some kind of letter to sell off the tow truck.
They bring me to the [INAUDIBLE], the Russian.
Was about 11 o'clock in the daytime.
Nothing.
I don't-- nobody asked me nothing.
I was sitting and waiting It was about 1:30 already in the morning, I am-- some man come over there, what called me in.
He called me, he says, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]-- black market, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].
He asked me.
I told him, I come from the other side of Poland to here, and I don't have what to live.
I have this letter what I have, and what I bring from Poland.
And I was selling this.
I want to sell it to be able to exist.
He let me out, and I was-- I was doing again black market.
I was careful.
It was-- There was so many people in Wlodzimierz or in Brest, [INAUDIBLE] or on some-- Lwow, was a lot of refugees living.
Is the Russian government make announcement, all this [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]-- they call it [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]-- to take passport, to get to be a Russian citizen.

The people was thinking what is-- we saw when the Russian come into-- to Ukraine, into Poland, they live a very hard life in Russia because we don't have-- the bakeries was open, you have to stay in the orchard to get bread.
Like the supermarket, you have to stay orchard.
People were staying a whole night in the orchard to get bread.
We saw harder life from the Russian people.
Is-- I will not say somebody-- nobody will take passport.
Is the Russian decided they will take all these people to send them away to Siberia.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] If you don't take a passport, they send away to Siberia.
This was already in 1941.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They bring wagons by the train, and they come in our town.
And they took-- all the-- all-- all they find the [INAUDIBLE].
They took them away to the-- to the train to load on top of the wagons.
They catch me with my two friends hiding over-- over an attic.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They uncover [INAUDIBLE] and he catched us.
He said, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].
You will go like this to Russia because you are black market man.
And they took us in a place, to ghetto-- where the trucks comed and they took the people to the train.
Is-- I was not far from where my brother lived.
Because I was the lucky guy and I runned, and I got out from this crowd.
And I was hiding with my brother.
And the other two, they went with the [INAUDIBLE].
They went to Siberia.
They lived the time from the war in the Siberia, all the time.
Although they come out.
They don't have easy, only they come home.
They was not used this work what that my friends told me.
They was in the woods cutting timbers.
It was very hard for them.
Only they live.
They come home.
Later, was taking a couple months, the Russian government announced again, all these people what they left, what they don't go to Russia or to Siberia, they have to take passport.
All right.
It was all right.
People were there that was hiding.
They give me a passport with the paragraph 38.
This mean you had to go away from the border 100 kilometer, about 60 miles, because no good for the country to keep you close to the border.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This was in '40, 1940.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I hear there's the town Kostopol not far from [?
Zdolbunov ?] on the Russian border, but there was before is we went with some friends where they were [INAUDIBLE].
We went over there.
We live over there.
And we did little bit again, little bit market.
Went to Vilna to Brest.
We bought something.
We sold.
We was turning around.
We was over there maybe eight months.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Later, I was once Saturday walking in the street, two guys, civil clothes comed over.
Come-- come with us.
They call me to the City Hall.
They bring me to City Hall.
City Hall, they told me, you're doing black market.
You will have to go to [?
Vilicka. ?] Over there is a mine for salt, and you will work over there.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
I come out from the-- when I come out from the City Hall, I start to ask the people what to do.
To [?
Vilicka ?] to got to me to work in a mine?
They told me, if you will take a job-- all the jobs was government jobs-- if you will take a job, you will stay.
You-- they will not bother you.
And so I went and I took a job in a factory from they make furniture.
We work over there a little while.
We get-- every day, we get 700 crumb of bread.
They give us just bread.
Well, only we lived.
We have-- I still have some little bit money with the friend.
Later in this job, I changed to go to work in a factory was to make the board, to lumbers.
The lumber.
We work over there.
Worked and worked.
Well, later, they said, the war broke out.
This was the 21st of-- this was the 21st of July-- the 22nd of July 1941.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This was when the-- the war broke out is we saw the Russian start to send military.
The Russian train was not mixing with the Polish train.
They have wider tracks.
Is the all soldiers, where they come in from the Russia to go fight the German, they come into [?
Zdolbunov ?] This was about eight mile from Kostopol.
And we saw they send so much military.
Eight days, the military was going to the border to fight the German.
Well, this was-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] it was day and night they was going.
This was not taking too long, maybe about two, three days later.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they start to go back.
It's a day and a half they went, and this was all the soldiers where they went over there in eight days.
They killed them because they went out with very hard airplanes, with a lot of tanks.
They send-- in this time from the war, they have about 3,000-- the German have about 3,000 planes, what they was fighting the Russian.
And right on the way, they killed all the people.
They went out and-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Was taking for a little while-- [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] was taking a little while, and the German comed in [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the 18th.
The German comed in, about four weeks later, they come into Kostopol.
Well, when the German was, we know it's not-- when the Russian left from Kostopol, is they said who want to go, they can go with-- with-- with the Russian Army to go to Russia.
And some people they went, and some people-- from our town, some boys went and some boys, they left.
We were staying in Kostopol.
We were staying and we saw how the Russian are living in the time when it was not war.
And now is war, what, we will go over there?
We were sitting and people talking, what can we eat?
They will send us to work.
We have to work over there, we will work for them.
What can they do to us?
We was not thinking it will come up to the gas chambers.
Never know.
All of the German come in.
They took out one Jew, a rich Jew, and they shot him behind-- they took him out a kilometer, a mile from the town.
They shot him.
They said they find in his basement ammunition.
They shot.
They scared them.
Later, they organized a Judenrat, a Jewish committee, and they start to send the people to work.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] They sent the people to work.
Here's the deal, you work in different places.
One day they send you here.
One day they send you here.
On the one day, they send me to work construction, that building.
And they make, the-- the man what he was managing over there, they make a gypsy.
This was for me very hard to take when a gypsy tell me what to do, and it was very hard work.
I was not be able-- as I was thinking, anyway I will get killed.
I will not go back to work.
I come for lunch.
We come home.
The Judenrat give us lunch, and I will not go back to work.
And I went-- and I went out.
I come home, and I told my-- I live over there by [NON-ENGLISH].
I was living there.
And I went out.
I was not home.
Two police come to look for me.
I said, I know what is-- if they was be catching me home, they was be killing me.
Only I was not home.
The other-- the other day, I went to the Judenrat.
You cannot stay home.
They send me to work.
We worked and make-- I should say they make a-- a street.
What they putting the Jews to work with the stones, heavy stones.
This was-- the street was-- everybody was crying about this work, everybody.
What can you do?
You have to work.
Well, we worked over there, I don't know, for how long.
Not too long, not too long.
And-- and it was a Friday, the 18th of August, 1941.
Is when we were in the Judenrat, it comes over a guy from the police.
His name was [INAUDIBLE].
He need people-- 10 people for the police to clean up over there.
They need 10 people.
They picked up 10 young people.
I was, at this time, a young man, not like now.
We come over there.
We worked over there.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We worked till lunch time.
Come lunch, we went to get lunch from the Judenrat.
And going back from the Judenrat-- going back from the Judenrat, I saw two trucks with German, and staying in the street.
Over there, we have just like a [INAUDIBLE], a German.
If you was walking in the sidewalk, he said to go down in the street.
If we walked in the street and we greet him, he hit you.
He walked on the sidewalk, you walked around always.
If you don't greet him, we walk on the sidewalk, they hit you, too because you're-- you're a Jew.
It was all that you can do to the Jews everything.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
We walked over there, and-- and we go back to work.
Coming back to work, and when we come to work, this [INAUDIBLE] is asking the [INAUDIBLE], who is a engineer?
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
No engineer.
Yeah, a couple of weeks before-- a couple weeks before, I was working with two of my friends in Kostopol in the [INAUDIBLE], in this place.
It's Saturday, we was working Saturday.
Usually we go home for lunch.
Only this was before one of my friend was married, and he lived with his wife in Kostopol.
He comed-- she comed over, and she said, don't go home because they catching people to work.
They catching people with trucks.
Don't go home.
And so we went out in the yard, and we sit down.
We took out a couple radishes, a couple tomatoes, and this was our lunch.
Going back from the lunch, we saw over there coming a Gestapo and-- and Ukrainian police.
As I said to my--
